---
title: "Unified Workflow"
subtitle: "Move from a stimulus to native features, optional alignment, and export without hiding the choices."
sidebar: concepts
---

## The workflow in one sentence

Give `extract_features()` a stimulus and a list of feature IDs. It returns the
extracted objects together with the plan and recipe that produced them. You can
then align selected objects to a named timeline or export them in a table.

The stages are:

```text
stimulus → plan → native feature objects → optional alignment → table or temporal payload
```

Here, “native” means that each object keeps the row times and spacing produced
by its feature-computing method. Only the first three stages happen
automatically when you call `extract_features()`. Alignment and table
conversion happen only when you ask for them.

## A small end-to-end example

```{python}
from natural_features.workflows import extract_features

result = extract_features(
    "one two two",
    features=["text.tokenize", "language.surface"],
)

{
    "outputs": list(result.features),
    "planned_steps": [row.step_id for row in result.plan.rows],
}
```

`text.tokenize` returns word events. `language.surface` returns repeated
numeric measurements for those words. Both are kept as typed temporal objects,
not immediately flattened into one generic table.

## What the result keeps

`ExtractFeaturesResult` is more than a dictionary of arrays. Its `features`
field holds outputs by feature ID. Its `plan` records the routes selected
before execution. Its `recipe` is the serializable step description, and its
`steps` field keeps the outputs of each executed step. It also carries known
input timelines and clock mappings.

This extra information answers practical questions later: Which extractor ran?
What parameters were used? Which intermediate step produced this object? Which
clock do its times use?

## Alignment answers a separate question

Extraction asks, “What did this method measure, and when?” Alignment asks,
“Which rows of that measurement belong to this other timeline?” Keeping these
questions separate prevents alignment from silently changing the extracted
object.

Here the language measurements are aligned to the word-event timeline:

```{python}
aligned = result.align_to(
    "text.tokenize",
    features="language.surface",
)

aligned.to_dataframe().loc[
    :,
    [
        "source_name",
        "source_row",
        "source_onset_s",
        "target_name",
        "target_index_start",
        "target_time_s",
    ],
]
```

The resulting `FeatureAlignment` stores the source row, source time, matched
target row, target time, clocks, and matching policy. The original
`FeatureSeries` remains unchanged.

## The four matching policies

| Policy | How a source row is assigned to the target |
|---|---|
| `overlap` | Match target intervals that overlap the source row's time support. |
| `start` | Use the source row's beginning time. |
| `center` | Use the midpoint of the source row's time support. |
| `nearest` | Choose the closest target time. |

Cross-clock alignment also requires a known clock map. A matching policy says
how rows relate; a clock map says how their time coordinates relate. Neither
substitutes for the other.

## Choosing the final handoff

Use a table when the next tool expects rectangular rows and columns. Use
`result.to_bundle()` and `FeatureBundle.temporal_payload()` when the next tool
can accept native feature timing and should make its own resampling or modeling
choice. The [Table interchange](table-interchange.qmd) and [Native time and
clocks](native-time-and-clocks.qmd) pages explain those two handoffs.

## When the workflow stops with an error

Unknown feature IDs or timeline names are errors. An object with no temporal
meaning cannot be used as an alignment source or target. An unknown matching
policy is also rejected. These failures make the missing choice visible rather
than guessing from row number or array shape.

The workflow does not select scientifically appropriate features, install
optional models, combine unrelated clocks, or decide how a downstream model
should aggregate several source rows. Those decisions remain explicit.

## See also

[Extract features](../start/03-extract-features.qmd) · [Align features to a timeline](../cookbook/timeline-alignment.qmd) · [Table interchange](table-interchange.qmd)
